words: SAM SCHRAGER: I think the first thing I was thinking of asking you, was why
your parents decided to leave Sweden, and come to America?
CARL OLSON: Oh. Well, I don't know if I should put that on the tape or not.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, its okay. No, do, do.

speaker: CO
words: Well, they come here for to get away from the militarists, capitalism, and

speaker: CO
words: religion. That sounds pretty bad, you know, in our country, don't it? Well,

speaker: CO
words: I'll tell you why religion is here. They run the country at that time. And

speaker: CO
words: the church had power over us, somethin' like the Catholics had, y'know. But

speaker: CO
words: they started breakin' away when they left, so now religion is just like it

speaker: CO
words: is here now, you know. Well, there was one kind there then. And you had

speaker: CO
words: to go to church, you had to do everything, y'know. They were boss, y'know.

speaker: CO
words: Practically run the country. And then the militarism. You didn't believe

speaker: CO
words: in wars, I guess—not very many fellers believes in that. But that's one

speaker: CO
words: reason. (He gets a picture.) See there, it was like it is here now, y'know—

speaker: CO
words: you're forced to go in the army. There my dad is. That'"was when he was

speaker: CO
words: in the army, see, in Sweden.

speaker: SS
words: Hm. So he had to serve?

speaker: CO
words: Yeh (chuckles). I don't know how, they went so two or three years, but

speaker: CO
words: it wasn't very long each year, I don't believe. I don't know for sure how

speaker: CO
words: long they had to, I think they had to spend a couple of years, anyway.

speaker: SS
words: What about the capitalism that you say?

speaker: CO
words: Well, you know, all the people were poor there, you know. They didn't

speaker: CO
words: have nothing. It was a pretty poor country, you know. Rocky, and just like

speaker: CO
words: goin' up in the mountains here maybe, y'know, about the same thing, where

speaker: CO
words: my folks come from. 'Course down there around Stockholm and down there, it

speaker: CO
words: was fairly good land, y'know. But that was all taken up. All the people

speaker: CO
words: owned that. And then, you know, the system was over there just like what

speaker: CO
words: they called it feudalism, if you ever know what that is. Well, if there was a

speaker: CO
words: family there, and when they died, the oldest boy got everything, see. The

speaker: CO
words: others didn't get nothing. And that's why it growed that way, you see, you

speaker: CO
words: got bigger and bigger and bigger estates. And that's the way it worked in

speaker: CO
words: Sweden at that time. But that isn't there no more, either. Feudalism died

speaker: CO
words: out, y'know, finally. They had slaves to do all their work, y'know. I

speaker: CO
words: think capitalism is on the way out too. Don't you?

speaker: SS
words: It wouldn't surprise me a bit.

speaker: CO
words: Well, here. If you go back in history, you know. First there was the real

speaker: CO
words: slaves, y'know. There was nothin' but slaves that worked for the people.

speaker: CO
words: Well, that come under the feudalism system, too. And that went on then, so

speaker: CO
words: long as there was room enough for the people, y'know, to live. And when that

speaker: CO
words: got too tough, then they got a rebellion, and they took it over. Then the

speaker: CO
words: kings come in and ruled, see, all over Europe—the kings ruled after that,

speaker: CO
words: after the feudalism did. And then the kings got taken out, so they went

speaker: CO
words: into democracy Row, you see, of course;-3 and it's capitalism here now, 100.

speaker: CO
words: And it can't last for these reasons. When they settled the United States,

speaker: CO
words: they settled on the East Coast, y'know. And when it got too tough for the

speaker: CO
words: people there, they kept moving west, see? They'd take a homestead and so

speaker: CO
words: forth, and they'd have a store, or a little sawmill. And all such, it was

speaker: CO
words: a future for them to go through, see. It's none here now, you know that.

speaker: CO
words: The small businesses are dying out all the time. The big chain store is

speaker: CO
words: takin' all the business, and big factories are takin' all the business there.

speaker: CO
words: And the big farms, you know where they're goin'—10,000 acres, you know, 2-

speaker: CO
words: 3,000 acres. Where are you gonna go? It's just a job, that's all you got.

speaker: CO
words: And that's gettin1 so rotten, that the union are ruining that now, y'know.

speaker: CO
words: Sure, go on strike, strike, strike, that's what caused the inflation now.

speaker: CO
words: If there're anything you're gonna guide, it's to guide the wages. I believed

speaker: CO
words: in the~when I was young, y'know, I believed in the union awful, because when

speaker: CO
words: I went out and worked, I had to carry my own bed, and sleep in an old bunkhouse

speaker: CO
words: see? No baths or nothin', y'know. Dust lay there, and stunk in them

speaker: CO
words: from the sweaty feet, y'know, they'd hang the socks up (chuckles). You couldn't

speaker: CO
words: hardly sleep from the stink. So they cleaned that up, y'see, the union. But

speaker: CO
words: now there gettin' so big that they're startin' to ruin the country. Ruin

speaker: CO
words: ing it, for the strikes. You know just as well as I do, there is a saturation

speaker: CO
words: point on everything, isn't it? It costs so much to make somethin*, and then

speaker: CO
words: your price is set there. Well then, if they strike, then that thing is gonna

speaker: CO
words: cost more. So they don't gain anything! when they buy it back, their

speaker: CO
words: money goes back, see. And what to do then? So, all I heard around here,

speaker: CO
words: "Save your money, save your money, so you don't go in the poorhouse," and

speaker: CO
words: so forth. What's the use in saving money if it's gonna go like this, now?

speaker: CO
words: The money I saved when I was young, two years, be wiped out.

speaker: SS
words: What you're saying is that the way it is here, it reminds you of the way

speaker: SS
words: it used to be in Sweden, when it made your parents come over here.

speaker: CO
words: Yah. So you see how that is. And that's the way it was over there at

speaker: CO
words: that time, because that country was settled all over, y'know, full of people,

speaker: CO
words: see, and there was no other place to go.

speaker: SS
words: Did your parents have a farm of their own over there?

speaker: CO
words: Oh, they had a little bit, y'know. They didn't have many acres, y'know.

speaker: CO
words: They had a coupla cows, and they used the old scythe, y'know, to cut the hay

speaker: CO
words: with. They didn't do any thrashing or anything like that, because, what,

speaker: CO
words: they didn't have fields enough, most of them. And that's the way they lived

speaker: CO
words: there, you see.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know if he had to hire out and work for bigger farmers?

speaker: CO
words: Oh well, they worked up north. I don't know if my dad did, but he was

speaker: CO
words: pretty young, y'know. He was only 22 years old when he come here. But they

speaker: CO
words: went north and done some logging, y'know, in the winter, and worked. But

speaker: CO
words: then, that wasn't very big, very much for all of them to do, you know. And

speaker: CO
words: it was pretty tough, too, y'know, when you get up north there. It's colder,

speaker: CO
words: and the days are short, and you can't put in more than five, six hours and

speaker: CO
words: it's dark, y'know. So they used to tell about that a whole lot. They floated

speaker: CO
words: the logs down the creeks, y'know, to the mills, and so on.

speaker: CO
words: But now Sweden has got a pretty good government, y'know. It's pretty

speaker: CO
words: good. You're taken care of 100 there, 100. If you ain't got a home to

speaker: CO
words: live in when you get old, they got a home for you. You ain't no villas

speaker: CO
words: and stuff like they have here, that pokes in a whole lot of sick people.

speaker: CO
words: Everybody thought that was so darn good, y'know, here, but it's no good.

speaker: CO
words: It's good, better'n nothing, y'know. But when the people gets broke, or he

speaker: CO
words: gets so he can't work, he's got to live somewheres. Well, then they poke

speaker: CO
words: him into those big homes, you see. And there's half-crazy people in there,

speaker: CO
words: and there's everything in there. They can't, they don't want to live that

speaker: CO
words: way. You wouldn't want to either, if you have pretty good shape, y'know.

speaker: CO
words: Well, they poke 'em all in one bunch. They shouldn't do that. You should

speaker: CO
words: have a, they should have a little house for 'em. And then have a house

speaker: CO
words: like they got here, and then put the people in that's got to be taken care

speaker: CO
words: of. Then they can live the way they want to. But before they only had a

speaker: CO
words: poorhouse here. You don't remember that, I guess? They had an old building,

speaker: CO
words: y'know, and they'd poke 'em in there, and it was ragged, and half starved to

speaker: CO
words: death, and fighting and quarreling all the time.

speaker: SS
words: They had those out here too?

speaker: CO
words: Yeh. I talked to a feller that come from down South. They had a poor

speaker: CO
words: house there, he said (chuckles). They had one out in the desert, y'know.

speaker: CO
words: There they got all kinds of people in it, he said. They got miners and

speaker: CO
words: farmers. And they were quarreling and fighting all the time, he said.

speaker: CO
words: And they'd run away, and then they had to run and find them, you know—

speaker: CO
words: mix up, you see, like that. That ain't no way to take care of people. My

speaker: CO
words: way of taking care of a country is two things they have to do. One thing

speaker: CO
words: they have to do is take care of the people. And if you take care of the

speaker: CO
words: people, the people will take care of the country. You'll never have any

speaker: CO
words: trouble in the country. And that's the whole secret. 'Course they're gettin'

speaker: CO
words: there a little better now, than they used to be here. Now we got Social

speaker: CO
words: Security and all else, y'know, that's pretty good. If they wouldn't have

speaker: CO
words: Social Security, you'd have to build 15, 20 houses like they got in Moscow

speaker: CO
words: to take care of the people. You'd be all broke. Well, you can see it your

speaker: CO
words: self. The inflation'd took all their money, see. See? You got to regulate

speaker: CO
words: things, 'til you got somethin' to jo by, the people. And that's why the

speaker: CO
words: young people, they don't know what they want today. They run all over and

speaker: CO
words: they don't know what they're lookin' for, because there's no future, you

speaker: CO
words: see. Dob, that's all it is left. And then the old tax system they got here.

speaker: CO
words: Tax your home. If you don't own a home, you don't own nothin'. You don't

speaker: CO
words: own it now, you're just leasing it. Paying so much a year to live in it,

speaker: CO
words: don't you? Taxes, that's same as a lease. Now they're getting so, well,

speaker: CO
words: we're paying 525 on this little thing here, taxes alone. Then we get an

speaker: CO
words: empty building, what happens? You lose it, or else pay taxes on an empty

speaker: CO
words: thing that there's no income from. That's what happened in the last depression,

speaker: CO
words: that's before you were born. Big building in Moscow there, y'know, a feller

speaker: CO
words: had named David, y'know, four stories high. Lost it on taxes. Then it was

speaker: CO
words: 4,000 taxes against it, and 4,000 then was same as 50,000 now, pretty near,

speaker: CO
words: y'know. So he couldn't pay it, and it sold on a tax sale. Pret' near

speaker: CO
words: everybody that had an apartment house in the '30's lost, lost 'em. Because

speaker: CO
words: the people that lived in the apartment houses couldn't pay the rent, see.

speaker: CO
words: And you couldn't kick 'em out. They had to keep 'em there. And they didn't

speaker: CO
words: get enough money to pay the rent on the damn thing, and keep it up. Rich

speaker: CO
words: people, millionaires went broke on having apartment houses. No, I'll tell

speaker: CO
words: ya, there's a lot of stuff here that's got to be corrected, if they ain't

speaker: CO
words: gonna lose the country.

speaker: SS
words: Let's go back and start with your parents coming over here...

speaker: CO
words: Well, they come over here in 1897. Come on a boat. They were six weeks

speaker: CO
words: on the ocean. And they had to come in what they called the steerage, that

speaker: CO
words: was the cheapest rate, y'know. And they lived way down in the boat then,

speaker: CO
words: you see. There's where she rocks. See, we get up here in the boat, on this,

speaker: CO
words: it doesn't rock so much there. Y'get way up, then it rocks again. So all

speaker: CO
words: the people that had money, they always took that place, you know. But that

speaker: CO
words: was a whole lot, co»st a whole lot. But they had to take steerage, way down

speaker: CO
words: the bottom. And they feed them, fed 'em, all right, but they had black coffee,

speaker: CO
words: my mother said, and a little bread, and that's about all. And the storm come

speaker: CO
words: up, and that old ship was goin' like that (tipping hand), and it laid over.

speaker: CO
words: They never thought they's goin' to make it across at all. And then when they

speaker: CO
words: got to Minnesota, my mother stayed there, and my dad come out here a year

speaker: CO
words: ahead, and took the homestead, y'know. And then when they come here, why

speaker: CO
words: it was no railroad here. It ended in Moscow. And he had to pack everything

speaker: CO
words: on his back from Moscow, that's 22 miles out to the ranch, and so forth.

speaker: SS
words: How did he happen to come to Idaho? Do you know?

speaker: CO
words: Well, it's because there was land to take here, you see. See, a lot of

speaker: CO
words: the Eastern stuff were taken. And it was some left on the prairie, just like

speaker: CO
words: Dakotas, ya know. But it's cold and windy, and no timber or no nothin', see.

speaker: CO
words: They couldn't even build themselves a home there, unless ya had money to buy

speaker: CO
words: lumber, see. But here you had the timber, see, and you could make a log

speaker: CO
words: cabin. That's what my folks did. They lived in that, y'see. And then you

speaker: CO
words: had firewood. So you see, it was whole lot of advantage to comin' out here,

speaker: CO
words: than go on the prairie in those days, unless you had the money, so you could

speaker: CO
words: build yourself a good home. And that's the reason they come out here, it

speaker: CO
words: was. They could've homesteaded in Nebraska, and Kansas, was quite a bit of

speaker: CO
words: land there, and in Montana, y'know, too. But prairie land, and cold winters,

speaker: CO
words: and blizzards so you couldn't see 50 feet, even. My mother said, when they

speaker: CO
words: stayed with their relatives there in Minneapolis—not- Minneapolis, but out of

speaker: CO
words: Minneapolis—the barn was about 150 feet away from the house, y'know. And

speaker: CO
words: they had to have a wire, and a rig to take ahold of, when they went out and

speaker: CO
words: fed the cattle in the winter, because if you didn't, you couldn't find your

speaker: CO
words: house. You had to take ahold of that thing and slide on the wire, to guide

speaker: CO
words: you (chuckles). Yeh. My mother said they done that when they stayed over

speaker: CO
words: there. They had to when a storm come up, they had to catch that rig and go

speaker: CO
words: out and milk your cows, and take ahold of it, and come back to the house.

speaker: SS
words: How did your father happen to find the homestead that he found? Do you

speaker: SS
words: know?

speaker: CO
words: Here?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: CO
words: Well, they surveyed the country, you know, and then they had locators, they

speaker: CO
words: called 'em. See, you'd stay in Moscow, y'know. And maybe they wanted five

speaker: CO
words: or ten dollars to go out and find a place for you that isn't taken. That's

speaker: CO
words: the way they found them, see, they had a map then. Then they'd mark on the

speaker: CO
words: map this is taken, and this is taken. And when the next man come, they know

speaker: CO
words: where to go with him, you see. That's the way it worked out. I don't know

speaker: CO
words: if they paid those locators anything or not. Maybe the government had 'em,

speaker: CO
words: y'know, free of charge. I don't know about that for sure. But I guess they

speaker: CO
words: had 'em free all right, maybe, so that you could help 'em out, y'know, to

speaker: CO
words: find a homestead. 'Cause the country, at that time, they want to grow, you

speaker: CO
words: know, so they helped the people quite a bit, that way.

speaker: SS
words: Were there many farms around him when...

speaker: CO
words: Well, after you get to the divide here, they call't, between here and Mos

speaker: CO
words: cow, that's halfways between here and Moscow. On that side was quite a few

speaker: CO
words: farms, Genesee country and all that, because that was open country, so you

speaker: CO
words: could get field quite quick, y'know, was no timber there, you know—all open.

speaker: CO
words: But here, in here was timber all over, just like you see it there now. That

speaker: CO
words: took quite awhile, y'know, to get a piece of field. You had to cut the trees

speaker: CO
words: down and let *em...You ringed the trees, they called 'em, big yellow pines

speaker: CO
words: like that? You'd take the ax and they'd cut a ring around it, cut the bark

speaker: CO
words: off, y'know, and then they'd die. That's the way, and then they died. When

speaker: CO
words: they got dry, then they bored a hole in 'em, and set a fire in 'em, and burned

speaker: CO
words: 'em down. That's the way they burned the tree up, too—all the way, a big

speaker: CO
words: tree down. They'd bore a hole this way, and then they'd bore one here, and

speaker: CO
words: they'd come together. And then they'd put some coals in there, and had the

speaker: CO
words: little bellow out here, see, to start the fire. And then when you got it

speaker: CO
words: started, it burned that log up, see. That hole here was the smokestack in

speaker: CO
words: other words, see. That's the way they cleared land. And then they used a

speaker: CO
words: lot o'timbers to make fences with, if you had a lot of it. They'd fall trees

speaker: CO
words: down, to keep the cows (chuckles) from running away. See, it was pret'near

speaker: CO
words: that high, and then the limbs, you know, and the cows couldn't get through.

speaker: SS
words: When did that area come by the name of Little Sweden?

speaker: CO
words: Well, there was so many Swedes here, that's why. They named it that, the

speaker: CO
words: people. You had names for everything in those days, y'know. You made 'em

speaker: CO
words: yourself, because the country wasn't settled. Now you say Moscow, and Troy

speaker: CO
words: road, and this road, and so forth, and Genesee country and that, y'see. And

speaker: CO
words: that's the way they had to name the stuff, so they'd know where they were,

speaker: CO
words: the people. See, we had names on everything on our farms. So when we talked

speaker: CO
words: to each other, we'd know where the folks were, or where they were goin'.

speaker: CO
words: Like the Big Point, the Little Point, the Mining Point, we had, that's our

speaker: CO
words: names now. East Around, North Hill, and North-up-in-the-Lane, we said—

speaker: CO
words: that's a road that runs there. If a man was comin*, we'd say, "There's a man

speaker: CO
words: comin', North-in-the-Lane," and all that. And named like that all over.

speaker: CO
words: That's the way they named. And the Mining Hill—where we had that mine, that

speaker: CO
words: was the Mining Hill. It was hills, y'know, that run out kinda ridgy, see.

speaker: CO
words: They named 'em Big Ridge, and Little Ridge, and all that stuff, so that they

speaker: CO
words: knew where they were. And that's the way all these other names were, y'know.

speaker: CO
words: Now, now we talk about people. Well, we had a neighbor August-by-the-Creek,

speaker: CO
words: see. See, he lived by the creek, and that's the way we'd know what or who

speaker: CO
words: they were talkin' about. So on, and all that kind of stuff.

speaker: SS
words: So what were the first years like for your parents out here?

speaker: CO
words: First years?

speaker: SS
words: First years.

speaker: CO
words: Oh, they were tough. Very tough. Because, well, it wasn't too bad after

speaker: CO
words: one year, because next year the railroad come, y'know. My dad worked on the

speaker: CO
words: railroad. So he made a little money that way, so he could buy a little stuff,

speaker: CO
words: But else it was pretty tough. I asked my mother why she didn't go back to

speaker: CO
words: Sweden? "Well," she said, "this country looks so much like Sweden, so we

speaker: CO
words: didn't get homesick." Because the living wasn't any better here then

speaker: CO
words: it was in Sweden, you see. 'Cause they had to start with nothing. Yuh.

speaker: SS
words: So it really reminded her of Sweden, this country?

speaker: CO
words: Yeh, it reminded her of the hills, you know, and the timber. Y'see, where

speaker: CO
words: we live out there, where we lived, it's pretty hilly country, you know. But

speaker: CO
words: it isn't sharp hills, it's big ones, you know, so they have big fields on all

speaker: CO
words: of it now. And canyons and draws, you know. And then there's a big creek

speaker: CO
words: run below our place, that we used to fish in all the time. then when

speaker: CO
words: we got enough money to get a gun, then we'd shoot a lot of grouse, y'know,

speaker: CO
words: to eat. We had a lot of blue grouse down there, canyon. We was huntin* all

speaker: CO
words: the time, y'know, fishing all the time, the creek too. No, no, no gamewarden

speaker: CO
words: to fool with you. You'd shoot all you could. My brother shot three birds

speaker: CO
words: with one shot one time, y'know. Three blue grouse. And then catch all the

speaker: CO
words: fish we could. Nobody to look at your basket like they do now. So that

speaker: CO
words: helped quite a bit. But the deer, they were shot out pretty fast. It was

speaker: CO
words: deer in here, y'know, but they bought the deer for 5 apiece, y'know. And

speaker: CO
words: those fellers had guns. They made a livin' that way so long as they could

speaker: CO
words: catch. They'd shoot 'em and take into Moscow and sell 'em for f5. So the

speaker: CO
words: deer was cleaned out pretty good when I grew up, there wasn't hardly any.

speaker: CO
words: But there's a lot of deer here now though, again.

speaker: SS
words: Yep.

speaker: CO
words: They move and go back, see, when they start loggin' in the mountains, then

speaker: CO
words: they scared 'em back. That's why they're here now. And then they a got limit

speaker: CO
words: on 'em, you know. You can't shoot more'n one.

speaker: SS
words: Tell roe how the country was different then in the woods then they are now.

speaker: CO
words: Different? How you mean?

speaker: SS
words: How it's changed. Well, when we were talking before, you were explaining

speaker: SS
words: to me why the creeks used to run, oh let's say year round?

speaker: CO
words: Well, that's because they didn't cut the timber off. See, the timber is

speaker: CO
words: cut off now all over. And the snow goes off right away, and the creeksJ  get

speaker: CO
words: way up, y'know, in the spring. Flows off. This creek here used to run all

speaker: CO
words: year, y'know. (Westfork Little Bear Creek, in Troy.) This isn't very big,

speaker: CO
words: y'know, but it'd be about this wide and this deep all year round. There was

speaker: CO
words: fish in it all the time. Now it isn't a drop of water in it, now. This year

speaker: CO
words: is awful dry anyway, but...That's what raised the hell, y'see. When the tim

speaker: CO
words: ber got cut off, and a little wind come, then the snow'd go just like that,

speaker: CO
words: y'know. Chinook wind will take it all off, y'know, 'cause there's nothin'

speaker: CO
words: to protect it but when the timber was there, then snow fell between the

speaker: CO
words: timber, and there was shade around them all the time. Up on Moscow Mountain

speaker: CO
words: there, the snow would lay off 'til Duly, y'know, before it was all gone.

speaker: SS
words: So the runoff was a lot more even.

speaker: CO
words: Yeh. Even all the time, see. Never got real, like it does now. You can

speaker: CO
words: see, in the spring when they get quite a bit of snow here, you go down and

speaker: CO
words: look at these creeks, and it's just pure mud comin' down. In those days the

speaker: CO
words: water was just as clear as a crystal. (Chuckles.) So now the country is go

speaker: CO
words: ing away too, y'know, on the top of it. You can imagine that this country

speaker: CO
words: isn't a hundred years old yet. And you go out and look and see what's happen

words: hundred years old, so you know how fast it's deteriorating. Y'know, they had,

speaker: SS

words: from being a little boy on the farm, and what the life was like when it was

speaker: SS
words: just a homestead.

speaker: CO
words: a town 'til I was 10 years old. I didn't know what a town was, 'cause we only

speaker: CO
words: had three horses on the place, and you couldn't get everybody to ride a horsethere

speaker: CO
words: wasn't enough. Ten, 12, kids, and three horses, y'know (chuckles).

speaker: CO
words: And it was nine miles, that's quite a hike. But when I got about 10 years

speaker: CO
words: old, we used to hike to town, y'know, and carry butter. And sell, y'know,

speaker: CO
words: sell the butter. And some days it was so hot, when we got to town, the butter

speaker: CO

words: There was no rock roads then, y'know. The farmers rode up and down with their

speaker: CO
words: wagons, y'know, and dust was this deep. Hot there, burn your feet off and

speaker: CO
words: go barefooted beside, because we didn't have enough shoes. So it wasn't any

speaker: CO
words: fun.

words: down around Moscow, they had a little breeze, I guess, but up here you didn't

speaker: CO

speaker: CO
words: (End of Side A.)

speaker: CO
words: live different now. There's a lot of kids in our family, y'see. So when

speaker: CO
words: one of them grew out of his clothes, the next kid wore it. So we had patches

speaker: CO
words: on everything, y'know, more before we throwed 'em away. They were real wore

words: out, y'know. And the same with the shoes, see, the next one, next one. My

speaker: CO

words: make a pair of pants for two bits, and you sewed it yourself, y'know. And

words: brick, and we sold them for a cent apiece. We didn't sell too many, but we

words: a stovepipe, because you might burn up your house, y'know. So they all put

words: in chimneys. And then he was pretty good with that broadax, y'know, cut

words: your log and make it about that wide, y'know. And then he'd dovetail 'em

speaker: CO
words: together in the corners. And they'd cut and make their own shingles. Get

words: a knife, you know, big, long, like this. And then they had a wooden club,

speaker: CO
words: they'd hit that, knock off a shingle, see.

words: (Chuckles.) And I remember, the nails when they first come out, they were

words: square, you know. I don't know if you ever seen any, have you? Yah. So my

speaker: CO

words: I believe, then. Square ones, most of them. They'd break easy. I don't

speaker: CO
words: how they made 'em, those days. They tapered all the way, you know, them

speaker: CO
words: square nails, they called 'em. But the round nails, they was sharp on the

speaker: SS
words: Did you say that you made your own bricks?

speaker: CO
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS

speaker: CO
words: Well, we had, your rig was about this big square, see (about five feet).

speaker: CO
words: And then in the middle of it, run a pole down. And you took old iron rods

speaker: CO
words: and stuff, and drove in that pole, see, all over, see. And then they had a

speaker: CO
words: sweep on it here, and a horse out there. The horse walked around this thing,

speaker: CO
words: mixed the mud, y'see. One of us would throw the mud in, and the other one

speaker: CO
words: stand there and take it when it come out, and throw it in the mold, see.

speaker: CO
words: Catch it by your hand, and throw it in the mold. Five, I think it made five

speaker: CO
words: brick at a time in that mold. And then we had a stick to smooth it out with

speaker: CO
words: on the top. And then we'd carry it out to the dryer. We had a place where

speaker: CO
words: you dried 'em, you know, boards they laid on. And that's the way we made

speaker: CO
words: them. One guy was shoveling in there, in, dirt all the time. And then they'd

speaker: CO
words: throw in so much water to get the mud the right thickness. But they weren't

speaker: CO
words: number one brick, you know, because—well, some of them were good.

speaker: CO
words: Then we had a kiln, and we'd set the brick in the kiln, you know, leave

speaker: CO
words: 'em about that much apart (about j inch). And then where the fireplace come

speaker: CO
words: in, come together like this, see, and then you'd put in more on the top. But

speaker: CO
words: in that fireplace there you put your wood in to burn 'em, to make 'em hard,

speaker: CO
words: y'know, see. And that's the way we burnt the brick. But they had to be reburnt

speaker: CO
words: too, you know. You'd have one kiln'd be about as big as this room

speaker: CO
words: here, and about that high (10 feet). And they'd set the brick all over there.

speaker: CO
words: And the top brick then, you see, and the one on the sides, they didn't get

speaker: CO
words: burnt enough. So that meant you had to throw the brick out, you picked out

speaker: CO
words: all the burnt brick, and reset it again, and then burnt 'em again. That's

speaker: CO
words: the way you had to get 'em hard enough. (Pause.)

words: I shewed you the picture here I had. Yah.

words: was in Moscow then, when this feller come up from Lewiston. So he tried it

words: and took a look at everything he could think of, and he went out and tried

words: it. When he turned to the left, why, yurop, or the right, either one, then

speaker: CO

words: So you go out in the haying, and put in 10 days or somethin'. Then the

words: And that's the way you got your work outside. I've worked on seven different

speaker: CO

speaker: CO
words: days, because you had to put the logs on a wagon, y'know. And you couldn't

speaker: CO

speaker: CO
words: a lot of wood down there. Was a heck of a wood town here, y'know. Gee

speaker: CO
words: whiz, y'know, 100 feet long, y'know—a wood pile laying by the railroad track

speaker: CO
words: to be loaded on the cars. Went to Spokane, some of it went to Moscow, and

speaker: CO
words: Genesee, and all that. It was the biggest shipping center between Spokane

words: name that way—for all the stuff they shipped out of here. Then they started

speaker: CO
words: gettin* fields, and then they had the wheat and oats and all that that come

speaker: CO
words: in here, and they shipped that out, y'see. So that's why it was such a ship

speaker: CO
words: ping town. (Pause.)

words: Yeh, and it was a rough town, too, you know, there was a couple, three

speaker: CO
words: murders in this town. That's plenty for a small town, ain't it? (Chuckles.)

words: And the sheriff to get shot, the policeman. And he was a mean guy anyway,

words: policeman. He lived right down where it happened. He was 84 years old, this

words: guy. Yah, Corrin, a feller named Corrin. He lives in, out of that little

words: Albion, Washington. That's above Colfax, y'know. Yah.

words: his back, and he fell (chuckles)» fell back, and there was old Hays that grab

words: bed him, and throwed him in jail. (Laughs.)

words: here, when he caught somebody that was drunk. And he put his own son in the

words: other one. And one of the boys was sittin' in the jail there. He said, "The

words: I don't know what he done, one of them. He was a barber, see, and they was

words: twins, so you couldn't tell them apart. So when the sheriff come down to

words: y'know. They skipped, get away, y'know, from crime. Two or three of them

words: tin' caught, y'know. I guess I told you about that feller that stole the pig,

words: huh?

words: Tell me that one again, 'cause I forget it already.

words: And then they went to work, I guess—I don't know if they went to work or

words: when we lived there, y'know. See, you didn*t have no riggins then. But

speaker: CO
words: the last war—or the first war—then they built a railroad down that canyon,

speaker: CO
words: and took pretty near all that timber out. There was a lot of yellow pine in

speaker: CO
words: that canyon, big trees, yknow. And they had a fire there too then, but it

speaker: CO
words: didn't do much damage. So I guess they sawed up more than theywasted, all

speaker: CO
words: right, when you figure it all out, yknow, in the mountains and the canyons.

speaker: CO
words: But it was a lot of wasted timber.

speaker: SS
words: About when would you say that these little sawmills started going out of

speaker: SS
words: business?

speaker: CO
words: Well, oh they been goin* out for the last...The last 10 years around here

speaker: CO
words: there hasn't hardly be any small sawmill. Used to be one up here in town,

speaker: CO
words: but that was bigger than they had in those days. But all those that sat up

speaker: CO
words: in the countries, they've been gone for about 20 years or more. Yup. But

speaker: CO
words: there were a lot of them, y'know.

speaker: SS
words: What did you know about this guy Vollmer?

speaker: CO
words: Vollmer?

speaker: SS
words: Vollmer.

speaker: CO
words: Well, he was here, y'know, and they named the town Vollmer after him all

speaker: CO
words: right. He had a lot of money, I guess. He was an Englishman, I believe.

speaker: CO
words: And then when they borrowed money, y'know, he'd charge about 12 on it or more,

speaker: CO
words: Then if you borrowed 100, y'know, you'd get only about 80, because he took

speaker: CO
words: the interest out, see? (Chuckles.) And the people didn't like him at all,

speaker: CO
words: y'know, so they lost the name. He lost his name here too, yknow, Vollmer—

speaker: CO
words: it was named Vollmer to start with. But they named it Troy. I don*t know

speaker: CO
words: how they happened to put Troy on, though, but they didn*t like Vollmer anyway.

speaker: CO
words: And then he went up on the Camas Prairie there, and there was a town named

speaker: CO
words: after him there, and he lost that too. He lost that name. They call it

speaker: CO
words: Cottonwood now, I believe. Yeh, the people didn't like him at all. 'Course

speaker: CO
words: a little jealousy there too, because he was rich, y'know (chuckles). Oh they

speaker: CO
words: didn't have much use for him.

speaker: SS
words: Was there a lot of helping out between the farms?

speaker: CO
words: Oh yeh, oh yeh. Yep. They done that, pret*near all of them, because they

speaker: CO
words: were all pretty poor, yknow, they didn't have anything. So they always worked

speaker: CO
words: together, helped each other, where they didn't have any boys. But the woman

speaker: CO
words: worked quit a bit those days too, you know. Take all the Swedish women, y'know,

speaker: CO
words: they done all the milking, y'know. That's what they done in the old country

speaker: CO
words: too, 100. The men never worked, never milked the cow in Sweden. So they

speaker: CO
words: done that, all these Swedes up here, the women done pretty near all the

speaker: CO
words: milking. And they worked in the field too, a lot of them, in the hay, hay

speaker: CO
words: business. My mother worked out in the field quite a bit. She'd even sow

speaker: CO
words: the wheat, by hand. Then my dad, he'd come behind with the harrow and harrow

speaker: CO
words: it down. They didn't have no seeders those days, y'know. So they didn't

speaker: CO
words: have much farm machinery, either.

speaker: SS
words: How did he harvest?

speaker: CO
words: Well, we had the thrash rigs come around, the old horse power come around.

speaker: CO
words: And then they had, after awhile they got the steam rig. But the first steam

speaker: CO
words: rigs they had here, they pulled the engine with horses. It wasn't self-propelled.

speaker: CO
words: Should have been here the other day downtown, there was a woman that stopped

speaker: CO
words: down on the bench there, and she had a whole boxfull of pictures. She

speaker: CO
words: wanted to know who this was, and this and that. And she had the picture of the

speaker: CO
words: thrasher, old thrash rig there and picnic picture. We used to have a picnic

speaker: CO
words: up here, this Nora Church, y*know. Every year the 24th of Dune, outside

speaker: CO
words: picnic, yknow. She had one of those pictures. And I pretty near forgot

speaker: CO
words: about that. But I dont know where she got all those pictures from, though.

speaker: CO
words: They were pretty old, a lot of them.

speaker: SS
words: You told me that the family had a mine when you were a kid.

speaker: CO
words: Oh, we done the mining, not him. We were the miners. Yeh, we done the

speaker: CO
words: mining. I showed you the picture of 'em here the other evening, didn't I?

speaker: CO
words: When we had that tunnel in the ground. Did I show you that?

speaker: SS
words: You showed me the picture. What I was wondering is if you'd tell me how

speaker: SS
words: the thing came about, and what you did with the mine?

speaker: CO
words: Well, you see that mine there was right next to our place pretty near, a

speaker: CO
words: half a mile away, see. A feller named Cox, he's the one that started it

speaker: CO
words: the first time. And he mined there, and nobody could take that land because

speaker: CO
words: there was mineral on it. See, you couldn't take a homestead then, if some

speaker: CO
words: body contested it for mineral. So there was 40 acres there, there's never

speaker: CO
words: been a homestead on it. It's still that way. So he mined and mined there,

speaker: CO
words: and he was a bachelor, and he had a shack. All he had for a stove was a

speaker: CO
words: whole lot of rocks, and he'd make a sourdough, and he'd put 'em in them hot

speaker: CO
words: rocks when they baked them (chuckles). And he was diggin' away there, and

speaker: CO
words: it was on the ridge like that, see. And they had to tunnel in here, A

speaker: CO
words: feller come down and asked him, "Well, have you found anything yet?"

speaker: CO
words: "No, no," he said, "if I don't find something pretty soon, I'll hit

speaker: CO
words: daylight," That meant he'll go through (chuckling). Well, he quit. I don't

speaker: CO
words: remember him very much. Then a bunch from Troy here took it over, and they

speaker: CO
words: drove a tunnel. And they had copper, a stringer of copper about that wide

speaker: CO
words: in it. And sank a shaft there, and then they connected Cox1 tunnel with this

speaker: CO
words: one, so you could go in one and then climb out through the other one. Well,

speaker: CO
words: they gave up. And then my brothers, they jumped the claim, and then we took

speaker: CO
words: it over in 1912. And we drove a tunnel about 300 feet ourselves. And then

speaker: CO
words: we drilled, core drilled it too, beside. But we didn't find nothin' either.

speaker: SS
words: What did you think you was going to find?

speaker: CO
words: Copper. It was copper stringers there all right, right on the top. But

speaker: CO
words: it wasn't big enough, y'know. They thought they'd widen out if they went

speaker: CO
words: down on them, you see. But they didn't, they seemed to hold the same. It

speaker: CO
words: was rich copper ore, but there wasn't enough. So we spent the winters there,

speaker: CO
words: you know, mining. In the summer we was thrashing, or doin' somethin' else.

speaker: CO
words: I was telling you about them two old fellers that said, "I never feel like

speaker: CO
words: greeting." Remember that?

speaker: SS
words: Tell it to me again.

speaker: CO
words: Well, there was two old guys there. They had a machine there, how to take

speaker: CO
words: the copper out of the ore, see. Leaching it, see. When you leach it, you

speaker: CO
words: got to burn it first, so the sulphur goes out, in the copper ore. And then

speaker: CO
words: they put it in the acid, and then they put iron in there, and the copper will

speaker: CO
words: stick on that, see. And they were experimenting with that machine. And they

speaker: CO
words: were halfway between our camp with that rig. And every time they come home

speaker: CO
words: from the mine they'd say, "One day closer to the grave, and I feel like greet

speaker: CO
words: ing," the old man would say. And we didn't know what in hell he meant by

speaker: CO
words: saying, "I feel like greeting," (Chuckles.) It sounded kind of funny, I

speaker: CO
words: thought. So he finally told us then. He said, "That's 'crying' on Scot.

speaker: CO
words: 'Greeting,' that's 'crying,' you're crying. Well, I feel like crying," he

speaker: CO
words: said. "Because you're workin' so hard, and I'm afraid you're going to get

speaker: CO
words: hurt down in that mine, see." So that's what he meant (chuckles). Yeh.

speaker: CO
words: Well, you know mining is kind of unsafe work, and we were pretty young kids,

speaker: CO
words: most of us. Not kids, but 15, 16, years old, I guess, three of us. And we

speaker: CO
words: had to drill the holes, and then put the dynamite in, and light the fuses.

speaker: CO
words: And then we'd come back next day, we had to dig that out, and haul it out.

speaker: CO
words: But one of my brothers, he was ,left handed, so he worked pretty good that

speaker: CO
words: way, you see. You see, when you're down in the tunnel, you know. Like this,

speaker: CO
words: the tunnel? (He frames the tunnel in a doorway.)

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: CO
words: See? Well I'd be up here drilling holes, see, and he was left handed, so

speaker: CO
words: he'd been on this side. And that worked out perfect. Then we'd drill a two

speaker: CO
words: holes here...

speaker: SS
words: You'd be each on one side.

speaker: CO
words: Yah. Then we'd drill the other holes here and they were uppers, drillin'

speaker: CO
words: this way. That was the easiest hole to drill. You stood and held your,

speaker: CO
words: you'd swing your handle there, and you'd drill them u. Do you know what they

speaker: CO
words: were for? The uppers?

speaker: SS
words: No.

speaker: CO
words: That's to break the rock out, so these holes would break out, and the lower

speaker: CO
words: ones would break. These holes go in straight.

speaker: SS
words: The high holes go in straight and the low holes go up?

speaker: CO
words: Yeh. The middle holes, not the lower ones. The ones on the bottom had

speaker: CO
words: to be straight, too.

speaker: SS
words: Ah, I see.

speaker: CO
words: See, we had six holes in it, see? And then when they light them with the

speaker: CO
words: fuse, and then run out of there, so we won't get caught. I think I've got

speaker: CO
words: a picture of that here, I'm lightin* fuses.

speaker: SS
words: You kids must*ve gotten a little tired of not finding, not hitting paydirt

speaker: SS
words: in there.

speaker: CO
words: Well, I never hit any paydirt in my life. I don't care what I tried (chuckles)

speaker: CO
words: I tried everything. We were in Montana and drilled for oil. Same thing

speaker: CO
words: there. You'd hit oil, there was oil there, but it wasn't enough. That's

speaker: CO
words: around Shelby, Montana. I never was lucky. Oh someday they might dig there.

speaker: CO
words: If they go down deep enough, it might be enough there, y'know, if they get

speaker: CO
words: down deep enough. And the copper mine, I don't know. But, it was fun. We
